compartment.title: 10 quota-saving enhancements in two passes

compartment: Traced rapid quota exhaustion after Antigravity 2.0 upgrade to 10 root causes across two passes. v1.6.20: thinking warmup opt-in (default false), capacity retries capped at 1, default thinking tier medium (16384 tokens), dynamic maxOutputTokens = thinkingBudget + 16384, per-message API request counter. v1.6.21: removed autopush from endpoint chain (3→2 endpoints), reduced empty_response_max_attempts from 4 to 2, added max_account_switches: 2, increased quota_refresh_interval_minutes from 15 to 30, replaced hardcoded 64K with dynamic computeClaudeMaxOutputTokens(), removed dead code path. Worst-case API calls per user message dropped from ~12 to ~5.

compartment.title: Root cause confirmation and quota_style_fallback

compartment: Confirmed normal operation is 1–2 API calls per message after all 11 fixes. Real root cause of fast exhaustion token consumption per message (thinking 16K + output ~8K + conversation history), not retry multipliers. Implemented quota_style_fallback config (default: false, env OPENCODE_ANTIGRAVITY_QUOTA_STYLE_FALLBACK) to eliminate double-pool quota spending when Gemini rate-limited — wired into resolveHeaderRoutingDecision() in plugin.ts. Lowered soft_quota_threshold_percent default from 100 to 90. Published v1.6.21. 1,126 tests pass.

compartment.title: Auth menu UX v1.6.22–v1.6.25

compartment: Four UX polish releases. v1.6.22: EXHAUSTED without reset shows `resets in ?h`, EXHAUSTED skips trailing `0%`, 100% READY groups hidden, consistent EXHAUSTED between badge and formatQuotaStatusPlain. v1.6.23: classifyOverallQuotaHealth() detects all-groups-exhausted, overrides `[active]` → `[exhausted]` in badge; condensed exhausted accounts to single "resets in 2h 30m" via formatWaitDuration(). v1.6.24: fixed duplicate "resets in" in badge and hint, inconsistent case (lowercase badges vs uppercase hints), non-sequential account numbering (storage index vs display position). v1.6.25: added `[limited]` (yellow) badge for partial exhaustion (some groups at 0% but not all); revised sort order: current → fully healthy → partially exhausted → fully exhausted → rate-limited → expired.

compartment.title: Per-model breakdown and two-line summary v1.6.26–v1.6.29

compartment: v1.6.26: blank-line tier separators, account summary header with count breakdown, disabled accounts sorted last (tier 6), extracted getAccountTier(), getHealthLabel(), buildAccountSummary(), buildAccountMenuItems(). v1.6.27: per-model quota exhaustion via buildModelBreakdown() — "Claude: 45 exhausted, resets in ~2h · Gemini: 12 exhausted, resets in ~5h". Migrated auth-menu.ts and cli.ts from local FingerprintHistoryEntry to canonical FingerprintVersion. v1.6.28: two-line summary (account counts line 1, per-model line 2); current account shows only `[current]` badge; [limited] hints strip aggregated reset times. v1.6.29: buildModelBreakdown() shows both available and exhausted counts per model.

compartment.title: Gemini Pro exhaustion and display refinements

compartment: Confirmed Gemini Pro models (gemini-2.5-pro, gemini-3-pro-preview, gemini-3.1-pro-preview) at 0% EXHAUSTED on all accounts expected free-tier behavior — Pro models paid-only since April 1, 2026. Antigravity OAuth path has separate Pro quotas; plugin prefers Antigravity for Pro models via resolveModelForHeaderStyle(). No code changes. v1.6.30: formatReset() shows `(unavailable)` instead of `(resetting...)` when remainingFraction ≤ 0 with past resetTime, indicating permanent free-tier unavailability. Explained display system: [limited] accounts work with mixed availability; unused accounts with future reset times reflect shared OAuth client-level quota pool; badge tiers based on per-group remaining capacity; reset times from Google API resetTime field.

compartment.title: Stale quota cache fix and misc v1.6.31

compartment: v1.6.31: Fixed stale quota cache in UI pipeline — 13 exhausted Claude accounts pre-check vs 1 post-check. Root cause: display path read cachedQuota from storage bypassing AccountManager staleness protections. classifyGroupStatus() returns READY when remainingFraction ≤ 0 with missing/past resetTime; classifyOverallQuotaHealth() skips stale-exhausted groups; buildModelBreakdown() excludes stale-exhausted from exhaustion counts. Accounts with only stale-exhausted groups show `[active]` badge. Addressed redundant account loading report as normal TUI select re-rendering (accounts load once from antigravity-accounts.json). Researched AntigravityManager (Draculabo/AntigravityManager, 1.8k⭐, v0.14.0) — standalone Electron app with SQLite, AES-256-GCM encryption; potential adoption: account import/export, encrypted credentials, desktop quota notifications. 1,131 tests pass.

compartment.title: Investigated AntigravityManager quota approach and implemented per-model tracking (v1.6.32)

compartment: In analyze mode with parallel research agents, examined AntigravityManager's quota source code. Found it uses the same Antigravity `fetchAvailableModels` API but with a different endpoint fallback order (daily-cloudcode-pa.sandbox.googleapis.com → cloudcode-pa.googleapis.com) and preserves each model's `remainingFraction` and `resetTime` individually. Synthesized recommendations to adopt the endpoint fallback chain and per-model quota storage. Implemented both: (1) `fetchAvailableModels` and `fetchGeminiCliQuota` now use the fallback chain with retry-on-429/5xx and retry-without-project on 403; (2) introduced `PerModelQuotaEntry` type storing each model's `modelId`, `displayName`, `remainingFraction`, and `resetTime` individually; (3) added `cachedPerModelQuota` to account metadata in storage, wired through `plugin.ts` → `cli.ts` → `auth-menu.ts`; (4) enhanced `buildModelBreakdown` to use per-model data for granular counts with group-level fallback when per-model data is not yet cached. All 42 test files pass, typecheck clean. Published as v1.6.32, committed, tagged, and pushed.

compartment.title: Analyzed Claude quota exhaustion and implemented per-account request tracking (v1.6.33)

compartment: Traced every fetch() path to diagnose fast Claude quota exhaustion. Found the root cause: each API call consumes 1 quota unit regardless of token size, Claude has low RPD (~50-100/day per account), and no per-account request tracking existed to debug consumption patterns. Implemented a daily per-account request counter: added `dailyRequestCounts` (`{ date, claude, gemini }` with auto-reset on date change) to `AccountMetadataV3` in storage schema; added `recordRequest()`, `getDailyRequestCounts()`, `getTotalDailyRequests()`, and `getDailyRequestSummary()` methods to `AccountManager`; wired post-fetch logging in `plugin.ts` to emit `[Quota] account=N family_today=X total_family_today=Y` debug output. All 1,131 tests pass, typecheck clean. Published as v1.6.33, committed, tagged, and pushed. U: check why the claude get exhausted fastly and how to debug that and calcul the request per account User requested root cause analysis of fast Claude quota exhaustion, debugging visibility improvements, and per-account request counting. The assistant identified the root cause (low RPD + no per-account tracking) and implemented the complete per-account daily request counter with storage schema, account manager methods, and debug logging.

compartment.title: Implemented remaining visibility features and explained quota model (v1.6.34)

compartment: Confirmed that `sticky` account selection keeps using the same account until rate-limited, which benefits prompt caching at the cost of hammering one account to exhaustion. Explained the Antigravity quota model: each API call costs 1 unit regardless of token size, Claude has ~50-100 RPD (requests per day) per Google account, and an active coding session at 20-30 requests/hour exhausts one account in ~2-5 hours. Listed possible enhancements (proactive rotation at ~80% RPD, request-aware sticky combining cache stability with smooth rotation, request deduplication) and flagged request-aware sticky as most impactful. Implemented all 3 remaining visibility features from the v1.6.33 proposal: quota consumption rate estimation (logs estimated time to exhaustion), post-request quota delta logging (remaining percentage and reset time per account after each request), and session-level quota summary (duration, total requests per model family, requests per hour, accounts used per request, plus previous session summary logged on new session start). Published as v1.6.34.

compartment.title: Investigated compute-based quota model and revised rotation strategy

compartment: User corrected the assistant's assumption of fixed per-account RPD — Antigravity quota is compute/token-based, not request-count-based. The `remainingFraction` from the API directly measures actual compute consumption, making it the right signal for rotation decisions rather than request counts. Quota operates on a 5-hour rolling session window (refreshes 5 hours after first request) with a 7-day hard cap if too many windows are exhausted quickly. An external article confirmed the three-layer cache architecture (system prompt → reference docs → history) and context compression as key cost-saving techniques. The assistant retracted its request-count-based sticky rotation proposal and revised to quota-fraction-aware rotation (switch accounts when `remainingFraction < 20%`). Three implementation options proposed: (A) quota-fraction proactive rotation, (B) smart account selection preferring highest-remaining-fraction accounts, (C) fix prompt cache destabilizers in `appendClaudeThinkingHint()`. No implementation started — user approval pending. U: you need to know the new rpd for antigravity, as know calculate based on compute verify that This directive shifted the approach from assuming a fixed per-account RPD to deriving it from real compute/quota data via `remainingFraction`, which directly measures the actual cost of each request to the Antigravity platform.

compartment.title: Fixed prompt cache destabilizers (v1.6.35)

compartment: User narrowed focus from three proposed options to only Option C (fix prompt cache destabilizers) and referenced the Antigravity Lab article on cache strategy. The article's principle — "stable stuff first, changing stuff last" — guided the fix approach. Investigated two cache destabilizer sites: `injectToolHardeningInstruction()` in `src/plugin/request-helpers.ts` used `parts.unshift()` which prepended the tool hardening instruction BEFORE OpenCode's stable system prompt prefix, busting the server-side prompt cache on every request where hardening state differed; `appendClaudeThinkingHint()` in `src/plugin/transform/claude.ts` was dead code (never called from `request.ts`). Fixed both: changed `unshift` → `push` in `injectToolHardeningInstruction()` so tool hardening appends after the stable prefix; also fixed the string systemInstruction path where the instruction text was placed before existing text, swapping order so existing text comes first; wired `appendClaudeThinkingHint()` into `request.ts` for Claude thinking models with tools, appended after the tool hardening block. Updated test expectations to match the new append order. System instruction order is now: OpenCode system prompt (stable) → tool hardening (constant text) → Claude thinking hint (constant text) → dynamic content (user input, tool results). All 42 test files pass, typecheck clean. Published as v1.6.35, committed, tagged, pushed, OpenCode package cache cleared.

compartment.title: Verified cache fixes with 3 verification layers and deployed v1.6.36

compartment: User asked how to verify prompt cache fixes are working. Documented 3 verification layers: Debug TUI logs (enable `debug_tui: true` in `antigravity.json`), response headers (`x-antigravity-cached-content-token-count`), and Magic Context diagnostics for HIT/BUST tracking. Confirmed all 3 cache destabilizer fixes from v1.6.35 are in place and passing (typecheck clean, all 1,131 tests pass). Also discovered that `applyClaudeTransforms()` is only called from test files — the production request path in `src/plugin/request.ts` calls the individual Claude transform steps directly, so the dual-call-site concern for thinking-hint injection does not cause double-fire. System instruction part order is now stable for prompt caching (original system prompt → tool hardening → thinking hint, all static content). Published v1.6.36 to npm — the build compiles cleanly, publishes, and the OpenCode package cache is cleared. The fix ensures the system instruction prefix is built via append-only operations with no mutation, making the prompt cache prefix stable across turns. Cache HITs are expected starting from the second message in a fresh conversation when `debug_tui: true` is set.

compartment.title: Compared against Antigravity Lab three-layer cache strategy

compartment: Fetched and analyzed the Antigravity Lab article on three-layer prompt cache strategy (system prompt with 24h TTL, reference docs with 6h TTL, recent history with 5min TTL, old history compressed to 200 tokens). Found the plugin is fundamentally a middleware interceptor that cannot implement the article's architecture because it does not own the request pipeline — OpenCode controls history, system prompt, and cache markers above, while the Antigravity proxy controls server-side caching and rejects Claude-specific `cache_control` markers below. Confirmed the plugin already implements the applicable subset: prefix stability (v1.6.36), `cache_control` preservation through transforms, `cachedContent` passthrough, and cache monitoring. The remaining wins (context compression, explicit `ephemeral` markers, TTL management) require changes in OpenCode or the Antigravity proxy — outside the plugin's control.

compartment.title: Investigated missing [Cache] HIT/MISS debug log lines for streaming responses

compartment: User reported missing [Cache] HIT/MISS log lines with hitRate in the debug output. Assistant investigated the streaming response path and found that successful streaming responses return early via TransformStream (line ~1767), never reaching the non-streaming `logCacheStats()` call at line ~1869. Traced the streaming transformer in `createStreamingTransformer` and found it processes `usageMetadata` only for synthetic usage injection — it does not call `logCacheStats()` from the streaming path, so all Claude streaming requests silently drop cache stats logging. No code changes applied. U: there is something missed like "Should see [Cache] HIT with hitRate > 0%" and "Should see [Cache] MISS — expected" The user confirmed the expected debug output format — the assistant traced the root cause to the streaming response path bypassing the non-streaming `logCacheStats()` call site, with no corresponding call in the streaming transformer.

compartment.title: Confirmed streaming cache stats dead code path and fix proposal

compartment: Continued investigation into missing [Cache] HIT/MISS log lines for streaming responses. Traced the exact dead code path: `logCacheStats()` at line ~1860 in `src/plugin/request.ts` is never reached for successful streaming responses because the streaming branch returns early via `return new Response(response.body.pipeThrough(streamingTransformer), ...)`. The streaming transformer at `src/plugin/core/streaming/transformer.ts` sees `usageMetadata` in SSE lines (line ~308-310) but only uses it as a boolean flag to decide whether to inject synthetic usage — it never extracts `cachedContentTokenCount`, `promptTokenCount`, or any cache statistics. Proposed fix: add `onUsageMetadata` callback to `StreamingCallbacks`, extract usage data from SSE lines in the streaming transformer's `flush()`, and wire the callback in `request.ts` to call `logCacheStats()` when the stream completes. No implementation started — pending user go-ahead.

compartment.title: Fixed dead cache stats logging for streaming responses (v1.6.37)

compartment: User agreed to proceed with the fix proposed in the prior investigation. The streaming response path returned early via `TransformStream`, never reaching the non-streaming `logCacheStats()` call at line ~1860 of `request.ts`. Added `StreamingUsageMetadata` type (cachedContentTokenCount, promptTokenCount, candidatesTokenCount, totalTokenCount) and `onUsageMetadata` callback to `StreamingCallbacks` in `streaming/types.ts`. Modified `transformSseLine()` in `streaming/transformer.ts` to extract usage metadata from SSE chunks into a `usageState` accumulator, and `flush()` fires the callback with the last-seen usage on stream completion. Wired `onUsageMetadata` in `request.ts` to call `logCacheStats()` with real token counts. All 42 test files pass, typecheck clean. Published as v1.6.37, committed, tagged, pushed, OpenCode package cache cleared. [Cache] HIT/MISS lines with cachedContentTokenCount and hitRate now appear for streaming Claude/Gemini responses.

compartment.title: Fixed 3 bugs and deployed v1.6.38

compartment: User asked whether remaining bugs existed. Assistant audited v1.6.37 code and found two bugs: `||` treating zero `promptTokenCount` as falsy (inflating hitRate), and dead `headers.set()` calls in the streaming callback firing after `Response` already copied the `Headers` object. User pointed out from debug output a third bug: `api_request #1` appearing for both the 429 and the retry 200, meaning `apiRequestCount` resets to 0 on account switch. Fixed all three: moved `apiRequestCount` declaration outside the `while (!shouldSwitchAccount)` loop so it persists across account switches; changed `||` to `??` in `request.ts` for nullish fallback; removed the 12 dead `headers.set()` lines from the streaming callback. All 42 test files pass, typecheck clean. Published as v1.6.38.

compartment.title: Systematic code audit confirms no remaining bugs or dead code post-v1.6.38

compartment: The user asked whether there were remaining bugs, dead code, or unused code after the v1.6.37/v1.6.38 changes. The assistant conducted a systematic audit of the streaming/cache stats pipeline across `request.ts`, `transformer.ts`, `types.ts`, `debug.ts`, and `plugin.ts`. Every area checked was clean: streaming `logCacheStats` uses correct `??` fallbacks (not `||`), non-streaming `headers.set()` fires before `Response` construction so it is not dead code, `extractUsageFromSsePayload` handles failed streaming responses with event-stream content-type, `hasSeenUsageMetadata` and `usageState.lastUsage` serve distinct purposes and are not redundant, `usageState` extraction in the transformer has proper `typeof === "number"` guards, and `apiRequestCount` persists across account switches after the v1.6.38 fix. No code changes were needed — the streaming/cache stats codebase is clean.

compartment.title: Investigated cache logging, quotaUser field, and QUOTA_EXHAUSTED detection from debug logs

compartment: The user shared debug logs and asked three questions about missing cache HIT/MISS lines, the `quotaUser=undefined` field, and whether the 429+QUOTA_EXHAUSTED handling is correct. The assistant traced each through the codebase and confirmed: (1) cache stats lines appear only after a successful streaming response in v1.6.37+ — the logs were from a session before the restart; (2) `quotaUser` is a deprecated field in the `Fingerprint` interface never set by `generateFingerprint()`, making it cosmetic noise; (3) Google's API uses HTTP 429 for both `RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED` (seconds wait, quick retry same account) and `QUOTA_EXHAUSTED` (hours/days, immediately switch accounts), distinguished by the response body `reason` field parsed via `extractRetryInfoFromBody()` in plugin.ts and `parseRateLimitReason()` in accounts.ts matching the `"QUOTA_EXHAUSTED"` string directly.

compartment.title: Verified v1.6.38 deployment and discovered pushDebug/logDebug logging split

compartment: The user restarted OpenCode and asked for verification. The assistant confirmed v1.6.38 is installed and active (npm registry + local cache both show 1.6.38), cache HIT/MISS logging appears in file logs with 66-78% HIT rates, and prompt cache recoveries after account switches demonstrate the v1.6.35 prefix stability fix is working. Key discovery: `pushDebug()` writes to the TUI thinking panel only (via `debugLines[]` → `formatDebugLinesForThinking()`), while `logDebug()` writes to the file-based debug log only via `createLogWriter()`. Cache stats (`logCacheStats`) use `logDebug()` internally, so they appear only in file logs — never in the TUI panel. The `api_request` counter also only goes through `pushDebug()`, explaining why it's not visible in file logs.

compartment.title: Added cache stats to TUI debug panel (v1.6.39)

compartment: The user reported that cache HIT/MISS lines don't appear in the TUI debug panel. The assistant traced the root cause: `logCacheStats()` calls `logDebug()` (file-only) but cache stats fire from `onUsageMetadata` during `flush()` after the stream starts, by which point `debugLines[]` for the TUI panel is already consumed. Additionally, `logDebug()` was never wired to TUI output. Fixed by adding `createLogger("request").debug()` calls alongside `logCacheStats()` in both the streaming path (inside the `onUsageMetadata` callback) and the non-streaming path, so cache stats now write to both the file log (via `logDebug()`) and the TUI log panel (via `app.log()` through `createLogger`). Published as v1.6.39 to npm.

compartment.title: Added previous-request cache stats to TUI thinking debug block (v1.6.40)

compartment: User asked how to verify v1.6.39 was active after restart. Assistant explained there's no startup version log and offered 3 verification methods (check package cache version, look for [Cache] lines in TUI, check api_request counter). User then shared TUI debug logs from a new session confirming v1.6.39 was running but showing no [Cache] HIT/MISS lines in the thinking debug block. Root cause: v1.6.39 added cache stats to the TUI log panel via `createLogger().debug()` / `app.log()`, but the thinking debug block is a separate sink built by `pushDebug()` before the stream starts — cache stats arrive after the stream completes in `flush()`, too late for the current request's debug block. Fix: added `lastCacheStats` module-level variable in `src/plugin/request.ts` with exported `getLastCacheStats()` getter, storing each request's cache result from `onUsageMetadata`. At the start of the next request's debug block, `pushDebug()` emits the previous request's cache line (e.g. `[Cache] HIT model=... read=111758 total=145000 hitRate=77%`). Message 1 shows no cache line (no previous data); message 2+ shows the previous request's stats. All tests pass, typecheck clean. Published as v1.6.40, committed (111758, 145000), cache cleared.

compartment.title: Verified v1.6.40 deployment in package cache

compartment: User asked to verify v1.6.40 was deployed. Assistant confirmed v1.6.40 is present in the npm package cache at `C:\Users\Expiren\.cache\opencode\packages\@expiren\opencode-antigravity-auth\package.json`, but noted the current session loaded the plugin at startup before v1.6.40 was published, so TUI verification requires restarting OpenCode and sending at least two messages — the [Cache] line appears starting from message 2.

compartment.title: Verified cache system working with 99.8% hit rate

compartment: User confirmed the plugin cache system is working correctly across multiple messages. Assistant explained how to interpret cache stats (HIT/MISS, read tokens, total tokens, hitRate) and confirmed consistency between the plugin's reporting and Magic Context diagnostics. The small rate difference (80% in MC vs 99.8% plugin) was traced to different token-denominator sources — MC uses a different usage field or excludes certain token categories. No code changes needed; the v1.6.35 prefix stability fix and v1.6.40 visibility features are verified working.

compartment.title: Investigated cache hit rate gap between plugin and native models

compartment: User asked about achieving 100% cache hit rate always; assistant explained it's physically impossible because each new message adds uncached tokens. User reframed to 95-99% as seen in non-plugin models. Assistant investigated the ~80% vs ~95% gap and identified root causes: Magic Context's zero-usage `message.updated` BUST inflation overstates misses, MC guidance injection changes the system prompt hash between turns, account rotation splits cache buckets, thinking block stripping changes content, and Antigravity proxy cache semantics differ. Conclusion: the ~80% displayed in MC is inflated by MC measurement artifacts — actual server-side cache hits already reach 99.8%. Most fixes require changes in Magic Context, not the plugin.

compartment.title: Diagnosed cache bust from Magic Context compression

compartment: Traced a cache hit rate drop from 100% to 34% between consecutive messages in the same session to Magic Context history compression. MC fires at 60K tokens / 40% of the 200K context window, rewriting the entire conversation history via compartmentalization. This changes the full request prefix, busting the Antigravity server-side cache (keyed on the full request prefix). Total prompt tokens dropped from 137K→26K with only the system prompt (~8,927 tokens) surviving as a cached prefix. Native models don't show this because OpenCode manages their cache markers at the SDK level above the plugin's fetch interception. Recommended raising MC `execute_threshold_tokens` to 120K and `execute_threshold_percentage` to 55-60 for Claude models to halve compression frequency.

compartment.title: Advised which MC config settings to change

compartment: Evaluated which Magic Context settings affect cache bust frequency on compression. `history_budget_percentage` (0.1) doesn't reduce bust severity because compressed text is still different regardless of how much is kept. `compressor.cooldown_ms` (600000/10min) is already fine with higher thresholds. Only `execute_threshold_tokens` and `execute_threshold_percentage` matter — raising them delays compression, reducing cache bust frequency without affecting other behavior.

compartment.title: Verified MC config changes applied

compartment: Verified user applied the recommended config changes to `C:\Users\Expiren\.config\opencode\magic-context.jsonc`: `execute_threshold_tokens` raised from 60K to 120K (default, Sonnet, Opus); `execute_threshold_percentage` raised from 40 to 60 (Sonnet, Opus). Noted the default threshold was also raised, affecting all models including Gemini/GPT — acceptable given their large context windows.

compartment.title: Identified remaining minor issues in codebase

compartment: Audited the codebase for remaining bugs after v1.6.40. Found two cosmetic bugs: `quotaUser=undefined` logged on every request because the deprecated field exists in the `Fingerprint` type (line 53 of `fingerprint.ts`) but is never set by `generateFingerprint()` or `collectCurrentFingerprint()`; the auth menu display path (`auth-menu.ts`/`quota-status.ts`) reads `cachedQuota` directly from storage without the ten-minute age gate or reset-time fail-open protections that `AccountManager` applies (already tracked in KNOWN_ISSUES). Missing features: `gemini-3.5-flash-preview` and `gemini-3.1-flash-image` variants lack `OPENCODE_MODEL_DEFINITIONS` entries (already tracked). The streaming/cache stats pipeline, nullish fallback operators, account switch request counter, and system instruction prefix stability were confirmed clean.

compartment.title: Investigated (unavailable) label contradiction for Pro models on gemini-cli pool

compartment: Traced why gemini-2.5-pro, gemini-3-pro-preview, and gemini-3.1-pro-preview display "(unavailable)" in the `│` tree quota display while the models work normally when routed through the Antigravity path. Root cause: `formatReset()` in `src/plugin.ts` shows "(unavailable)" when `remainingFraction ≤ 0` AND `resetTime` is in the past (treating it as permanently paywalled), but `classifyGroupStatus()` returns `[READY]` for the same condition (fail-open). This creates a UI contradiction — the badge says usable, the label says permanently unavailable. The Pro models correctly show 0% on the gemini-cli pool (Pro-only/paid there), but the plugin routes Pro models through the Antigravity path where separate quota exists. Four fix options presented: hide permanently-0% models from gemini-cli section, change label to "(not on this pool)", cross-reference pools before displaying unavailable, or remove the label entirely. No code changes applied — awaiting user choice. U: why is it "(unavailable)" even i dont use them when i can use them in opencode and its show me that, when it works in other apps like the "Antigravity Manager" The user's question drove the investigation into the formatReset logic, revealing that the same data condition (0% remaining + past resetTime) produces `[READY]` from classifyGroupStatus() but `(unavailable)` from formatReset() — a UI contradiction where models that route to a different quota pool are labeled permanently unavailable.

compartment.title: Traced cache hit rate plateau to account rotation, not MC compression

compartment: User reported cache hit rate stuck at 76-78% instead of the 90-99% seen earlier in the session. The first analysis incorrectly attributed this to MC compression creating a new cache epoch. User provided the full session log, leading to a corrected root cause: 429 QUOTA_EXHAUSTED forces involuntary account switches, and each switch splits the server-side cache bucket. Returning to a previously-used account finds a stale cache (111K tokens cached vs 141-146K total conversation = 76-79% hit) because the conversation grew while the account was idle. Each new account starts at 0% hit rate (cold cache) and takes 2-3 messages of consistent account use to warm back to 95-99%. The sticky strategy already minimizes voluntary switches, but 429s cannot be avoided — every forced switch costs at least one cache miss and degrades return-to-old-account performance. U: now why cant arrive to 99 or 100 or 90 or 95 as before ? The user's question drove the trace. The first answer (MC compression) was incorrect — the real cause is the stale-cache-on-return effect after every 429-forced account switch, as confirmed by the full session log.

compartment.title: Explained v1.6.40 one-message delay causing apparent consecutive cache MISSes after account switch

compartment: The user asked why two consecutive debug blocks both showed cache MISS on the newly switched account (idx=6) after a 429-forced rotation. The assistant traced the v1.6.40 one-message delay: the `[Cache]` line in debug block N shows the **previous** request's stats, not the current one. Block 1's MISS (read=0 total=144163) reports the 429 on account 1. Block 2's MISS (read=0 total=145759) reports Block 1's result — the first request on account 6, correctly a cold MISS on the new cache bucket. The second request on account 6's own cache result would only appear in Block 3 (not provided). The apparent two consecutive MISSes is actually one MISS displayed twice (once live, once as lagged history). The second request on account 6 may have been a HIT but cannot be confirmed without Block 3's debug output.

compartment.title: Identified fundamental tension between per-account quota exhaustion and server-side cache warmup

compartment: The user asked why account idx=6 showed consecutive 0% MISSes after a 429-forced rotation. The assistant traced this to Google's implicit prefix caching requiring multiple requests (5-20+) with the same prefix before activating — it does not cache eagerly on first or second request. With accounts exhausting after ~5-7 requests (claude_today counters), most accounts never reach cache warmth. This was confirmed by comparing cache hit rates: account 52 (20+ requests) had 99% HIT with 150K cached tokens, while account 6 at 3-4 requests remained at 0% MISS. Implementation-level fixes require either accounts with higher quota (more requests before exhaustion) or fewer accounts with more quota per account.

compartment.title: Verified MC and plugin cache are independent through MC log analysis

compartment: The user directed investigation into whether Magic Context and the plugin cache interact. The assistant analyzed Magic Context logs from the session, correlating MC events with plugin cache stats. Key findings: MC transforms are cache-stable — the cache stayed at 150,978 tokens for 5+ consecutive messages while MC performed per-turn structural noise stripping (18-41 parts) and guidance injection. MC uses sentinel replacement (`.map()` not `.filter()`), preserving array indices so the prefix hash doesn't change. MC's guidance injection is also stable (same `ctxReduce=true, subagent=false` parameters every turn). The only cache bust coinciding with MC activity was at 22:56:55 when compartmentalization and an account switch happened simultaneously — either alone would have busted the cache. MC and the plugin operate at different layers (MC modifies the request body content; the plugin modifies auth/headers/endpoint) and don't share state — both independently affect the server-side cache key. Account rotation from QUOTA_EXHAUSTED is the sole persistent cache problem: each new account starts at 0% cold cache and exhausts in ~5-7 requests, never reaching the cache warmup threshold. The assistant identified pre-warming cache on new accounts via a lightweight probe request as a potential plugin-level fix. U: We need to verify if there's a relation between magic context and our plugin for cache, check the magic context logs User directed investigation into whether MC and the plugin share cache state, using MC logs as evidence. The assistant analyzed MC logs and confirmed the two systems are independent.

compartment.title: Investigated account rotation fix — presented three options

compartment: User asked how to fix or limit account rotation to avoid cache busts from forced switches. Agent investigated `accounts.ts` and `plugin.ts`, finding the current selection logic uses `scheduling_mode: cache_first` (60s wait) with `max_account_switches: 2` and `sessionUsedAccounts` tracking. Three options presented: (A) cache-aware rotation preferring session-used accounts for warm cache, (B) increase `max_cache_first_wait_seconds` for `RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED` only, (C) reduce active account pool. Recommended Option A as most impactful — returning accounts have ~76-79% partial cache vs 0% cold cache on fresh accounts. No implementation started — awaiting user decision. U: ok how we can fix or limit the account rotation so we avoid that or make it more strict. User directed the investigation toward fixing the account rotation problem specifically for cache stability. Agent analyzed the selection logic and presented three options with a recommendation.

compartment.title: Researched external solutions for multi-account cache optimization

compartment: User didn't choose an option and asked the agent to research online. Agent ran 4 parallel research agents covering Google implicit prefix caching docs, multi-account LLM proxy cache strategies, Antigravity Lab caching article, and Google Gemini explicit context caching API. Research confirmed: cache is per-account (OAuth-identity-scoped), explicit `cachedContents.create` is unavailable for OAuth users on Antigravity endpoints, cross-account cache sharing is impossible. Three strategies identified ranked by impact: (1) warmup probe sends a lightweight `maxOutputTokens: 1` request with the same prefix to pre-warm the new account's cache on forced switch, (2) prefer previously-used accounts for ~76-79% partial cache vs 0% cold, (3) proactive rotation switches when `remainingFraction` drops below 10-15% to avoid panic 429s. Recommended implementation order: 1→2→3. Caveated that Antigravity proxy may need 2-3 requests for cache activation (not just 1 as Google docs claim), so warmup may need multiple probes. U: i dont know what best one, can you check on internet about that and about our plugin setuation and about the caching and etc. and multiple accounts, may be you will get solution about this issues User directed the agent to seek external research instead of choosing among internal options. Four parallel research agents ran and synthesized findings into three ranked strategies with recommended implementation order.

compartment.title: Recommended 3-strategy cache optimization

compartment: User asked for recommendation on using all three cache optimization strategies together (warmup probe, prefer used accounts, proactive rotation). Analyzed the combined lifecycle coverage across Google's implicit prefix caching architecture: Strategy 1 avoids cold cache on new accounts via lightweight probe, Strategy 2 returns ~76-79% partial cache vs 0% cold on forced 429 rotation by preferring session-used accounts, Strategy 3 pre-switches before hitting 429 entirely by monitoring remainingFraction. Each covers a different stage — together they eliminate cold caches and 429-forced switches. Recommended implementing in order 1→2→3 with build-test-publish-increment after each. User agreed.

compartment.title: Implemented all three cache optimization strategies (v1.6.41–v1.6.43)

compartment:
Implemented all three strategies incrementally with build-test-publish between each.
Strategy 1 (v1.6.41): Added `cache_warmup_probe` config option to `src/plugin/config/schema.ts` (default true). Implemented `runCacheWarmupProbe()` in `src/plugin.ts` that fires on the first `fetch()` for each account, sending a probe with `maxOutputTokens: 1` and the same model/system/messages prefix to pre-warm the server-side cache. Probe skipped when `cache_warmup_probe` is false or `scheduling_mode === "capacity"`.
Strategy 2 (v1.6.42): Added `sessionUsedAccounts` Set to `AccountManager` in `src/plugin/accounts.ts` tracking which accounts have been used this session. Modified `getNextForFamily()` to prefer session-used accounts (warm cache ~76-79% partial hit) over fresh accounts (0% cold) on forced 429 rotation. Wired `recordSessionUsage()` in `src/plugin.ts` after account selection.
Strategy 3 (v1.6.43): Added `proactive_rotation_threshold_percent` config to `src/plugin/config/schema.ts` (default 20, 0 to disable). Implemented `proactivelyRotateForFamily()` in `AccountManager` checking `remainingFraction` against threshold and switching to a warm-cache account with highest remaining quota. Wired post-successful-response detection in `src/plugin.ts` — after each successful API response, if quota drops below threshold, switches accounts immediately to avoid future 429s. Added `resolveQuotaGroup` import.
All 42 test files pass after each strategy. Published v1.6.41–v1.6.43 to npm, committed and pushed to origin, OpenCode package cache cleared. Provided comprehensive summary: Strategy 1 seeds cache on first account use (`[WarmupProbe]`), Strategy 3 proactively rotates before 429 (`[ProactiveRotation]`), Strategy 2 picks warmest target on forced 429. Config: `cache_warmup_probe: true`, `proactive_rotation_threshold_percent: 20`.

compartment.title: Testing verification instructions

compartment: User wants to verify the deployed strategies are working without bugs. Assistant provided step-by-step instructions: restart OpenCode (not in this cached session), ensure `debug_tui: true` in `C:\Users\Expiren\.config\opencode\antigravity.json`, start a fresh conversation, send 3-5 messages and watch the TUI debug panel for `[WarmupProbe]` on first account use, `[Cache] HIT hitRate=X%` on message 2+, `[ProactiveRotation]` when quota drops below 20%, and warm-cache preference on 429 rotation. User to share debug output for verification.

compartment.title: Fixed warmup probe 400 bug (v1.6.44)

compartment: User restarted OpenCode and tested cache strategies in a fresh conversation. The assistant analyzed the debug TUI output and found the warmup probe was returning HTTP 400 on account switch. Root cause: the probe body included `candidateCount: 1` (unsupported by Antigravity API) and `thinkingBudget: 0` (invalid for thinking-required Claude models — Antigravity rejects zero budgets). Fixed both: removed `candidateCount`, changed `thinkingBudget` to `128` (minimal positive value that Antigravity accepts). The other two strategies were verified working: warm-cache selection correctly preferred session-used accounts on forced 429 rotation (account 9 → 49 with `warmup=true`), and proactive rotation was correctly not triggered (account 49 had only 4 requests with quota well above 20%). Cache recovery was excellent despite the broken probe — hit rate went 79% → 93% → 93% → 100% across 4 messages. Published v1.6.44, committed, tagged, pushed, and cleared OpenCode package cache.

compartment.title: Post-v1.6.44 testing session analysis

compartment: User tested the three cache optimization strategies in a fresh session (account 7, Claude). No 429 occurred because account 7 had sufficient quota, so cache-warmup-probe, account-switch, and proactive rotation were not exercised. Sticky selection stayed on account 7 the entire session. Cache warmed naturally (0%→93%→100% over 3 messages). Assistant confirmed all strategies that did activate were working correctly.

compartment.title: Warmup probe 400 persisted - v1.6.45 fix for thinkingConfig contradiction

compartment: User reported that cache-warmup-probe still returned 400 even after claiming v1.6.44 was running. Investigation confirmed the v1.6.44 fix (removed candidateCount, set thinkingBudget: 128) was in the published build but the root cause was misdiagnosed. The probe body `maxOutputTokens: 1` combined with `thinkingBudget: 128` creates a contradiction — the API requires maxOutputTokens >= thinkingBudget, making 1 < 128 invalid. Fixed by stripping thinkingConfig entirely from the probe, replacing generationConfig with just `{ maxOutputTokens: 1 }`. Published v1.6.45 with all tests passing.

compartment.title: Warmup probe still 400 - v1.6.46 fix uses streaming endpoint

compartment: User tested v1.6.45 but the probe still returned 400. Investigation confirmed the generationConfig fix was present in the published build, but the probe fetches the non-streaming `:generateContent` endpoint while the Antigravity v1internal API does not support non-streaming for Claude models — only `:streamGenerateContent?alt=sse` works. Fixed by using the same streaming URL as the real request, setting `maxOutputTokens: 1` with no thinkingConfig, and draining the SSE stream response body via a reader loop. Published v1.6.46.

compartment.title: Explained cache_warmup_probe token and quota impact

compartment: The user asked whether the cache_warmup_probe (v1.6.41, streaming endpoint v1.6.46) affects cache or quota. The assistant explained the probe sends the full conversation prefix as input tokens but only generates one output token — it consumes quota as a real API call but saves more than it costs when the account handles 5+ subsequent requests due to server-side cache HITs. The user clarified they were asking about token cost specifically; the assistant confirmed the probe costs fewer tokens than a normal request since output generation is virtually zero, and disabling with `cache_warmup_probe: false` eliminates the probe entirely.

compartment.title: Warmup probe still 400 — v1.6.47 restores thinkingConfig and adds error body logging

compartment: User shared debug output showing `cache-warmup-probe: done status=400` persisting after v1.6.46's streaming endpoint fix. Assistant verified v1.6.46 was deployed and confirmed the streaming URL and `maxOutputTokens: 1` with no `thinkingConfig` were correctly compiled into the bundle. Hypothesized that Claude thinking models require `thinkingConfig` to be present — omitting it entirely is invalid for those models. Published v1.6.47 with two changes: (1) restored `thinkingConfig` for thinking models with `thinkingBudget: 128` and raised `maxOutputTokens: 256` (must be ≥ thinkingBudget, fixing the v1.6.44 contradiction), while non-thinking models still get just `maxOutputTokens: 1`; (2) added response body logging so if the probe still returns 400, the debug panel shows `cache-warmup-probe: done status=400 error=<first 200 chars>` for targeted diagnosis. Committed, tagged, pushed, OpenCode package cache cleared.

compartment.title: Clarified cache gap behavior on single-account returns

compartment: Explained that a 74% cache hit rate on a single account is caused by returning after the conversation grew while on other accounts. On a single account without switches, cache should reach ~97-98%. Confirmed there is no way to persist or pin Google's per-identity server-side cache — the explicit `cachedContents.create` API is unavailable for Antigravity OAuth users, and cache is implicitly managed by Google based on repeated prefix patterns.

compartment.title: Traced same-account cache oscillation (74%→100%→74%) to Magic Context compression

compartment: The user noticed cache hit rate oscillating 74%→100%→74% on the same account (idx=64) across three consecutive debug blocks. The assistant traced the root cause using the v1.6.40 one-message delay structure: total tokens dropped from 150K to 120K between requests (a Magic Context compression event). The compressed prefix happened to exactly match a cached entry (100% at 120K total), then the next message grew back to 151K and the server hit the older pre-compression cache entry (111K cached of 151K total = 74%). The two cache entries coexist on Google's server and the server alternates between them based on which prefix matches better for each request. Confirmed the plugin's prefix is stable (v1.6.35 fix) — the oscillation is entirely an MC-level artifact. Without MC compression, the same account would achieve 97-99% hit rate consistently. No code changes were made.

compartment.title: Investigated MC cache oscillation, fixed warmup probe (v1.6.48)

compartment: Investigated whether the analyze-mode template originates from Magic Context. Three research agents independently confirmed MC uses `experimental.chat.messages.transform` (Phase 1, before the plugin's fetch interception) to rewrite conversation history and inject system instructions — this is the mechanism for the analyze-mode template and the root cause of cache oscillation. During parallel research, agents discovered the warmup probe was failing with 400 because `prepareAntigravityRequest()` already transforms the body to v1internal format, which has no `generationConfig` field — injecting `generationConfig` caused `Unknown name "generationConfig": Cannot find field`. Fixed (v1.6.48) by sending the actual request body as-is through the streaming endpoint and aborting after the first SSE chunk, eliminating all custom body injection (`generationConfig`, `thinkingConfig`, `maxOutputTokens`). Agents also confirmed the 74%↔100% cache oscillation is caused by MC history compression creating two coexisting server-side cache entries (post-compression ~81K prefix, pre-compression ~113-117K prefix) that Google's server alternates between — unfixable in the plugin because MC modifies the message array before the plugin sees it.

compartment.title: MC cache-busting diagnosis and config recommendations

compartment: The user shared their magic-context.jsonc config and asked what causes cache loss in Magic Context. Four parallel research agents analyzed the MC codebase (scheduler.ts, execute pass triggers, system prompt transforms, message transform pipeline) and identified how MC decides to execute (cache bust) vs defer (cache stable). Three execute triggers found: context usage ≥ threshold (60%/120K tokens), cache TTL expired (59m), and force materialize (hardcoded 85%). Seven config issues were identified and recommended: (1) fix cache_ttl.default typo "59mm" → "59m", (2-3) raise execute_threshold_percentage from 60 to 75 and execute_threshold_tokens from 120K to 150K for Claude models, (4) raise history_budget_percentage from 0.1 to 0.15, (5) raise compressor.cooldown_ms from 600K to 900K, (6) consider switching sidekick model to avoid Antigravity Gemini quota drain, (7) raise GPT/Gemini thresholds from 20-35% to 50-60%. The user asked twice whether that was everything; the second round added items 6-7. Recommendations were presented but not applied.

compartment.title: 503/429 trace and warmup probe fix (v1.6.49)

compartment: The user shared debug logs showing a 503 (MODEL_CAPACITY_EXHAUSTED, server capacity) followed by 429 (QUOTA_EXHAUSTED) on the same account. Tracing found the 503 retry consumed a quota unit, accelerating quota exhaustion. The warmup probe was still returning 400 with "Unknown name generationConfig" because prepareAntigravityRequest() produces a body containing generationConfig that the v1internal streaming endpoint rejects. The user corrected the assistant's assumption that quota is request-count-based — Antigravity quota is compute/token-based with no fixed RPD, and claude_today is just a request counter, not a limit. Implemented v1.6.49: strips all output-related config (generationConfig, thinkingConfig, tools, toolConfig, safetySettings) from the probe body before sending, keeping only contents + systemInstruction. Cache warming only needs the input prefix to match the server-side cache key; output config is irrelevant. Probe sends through the streaming endpoint, reads the first SSE chunk, and aborts. All 42 test files pass, typecheck clean. Published, committed, tagged, pushed, OpenCode package cache cleared.

compartment.title: MC config threshold regression diagnosed and reverted

compartment: User tested the raised MC thresholds (75%/150K recommended in compartment 1414-1428) and cache hit rate dropped from 80%+ to 71%. The assistant analyzed the math: higher thresholds delay compression → conversation grows larger between compressions → cached prefix stays the same (112K) → lower hit rate percentage, and when MC finally executes, the bust is more severe. Assistant retracted the recommendation. Reverting to original config (60%/120K) with only the "59mm"→"59m" typo fix kept.

compartment.title: v1.6.49 deployment verified, probe fix pending 429 test

compartment: User shared debug showing warmup probe still 400 from old v1.6.48 still loaded at startup. After restarting OpenCode, v1.6.49 confirmed active. New session shows normal cache progression: no cache line on first message (v1.6.40 one-message delay), MISS (0%) on second, then HIT at 76% climbing. Warmup probe did not fire because no account switch occurred (sticky on account 10 throughout). The v1.6.49 fix — stripping `generationConfig`, `thinkingConfig`, `tools`, `toolConfig`, `safetySettings` from the probe body — can only be exercised when a 429 QUOTA_EXHAUSTED forces an account switch. User is reverting MC config changes incrementally (one value at a time, testing between each).

compartment.title: Verified v1.6.49 warmup probe working in live session

compartment: The user shared debug TUI logs from a fresh OpenCode session showing account switches with cache-warmup-probe. The assistant analyzed the timeline across accounts 10→14→16 and confirmed: the probe returns status=200 (aborted after first chunk) on each account switch, and the first real request on the new account achieves 98% HIT (read=138512 total=140695) — previously 0% MISS before the v1.6.49 probe fix. Cache then drops to ~79% after Magic Context compression creates an older coexisting server-side cache entry (112K cached vs 138K from the probe-seeded prefix). The user questioned whether the probe was working; the assistant re-analyzed and confirmed the probe itself works correctly — the observed 78-79% hit rate is caused by MC compression creating alternating cache buckets (138K vs 112K), not by probe failure. The v1.6.49 approach of stripping output-related config (generationConfig, thinkingConfig, tools, toolConfig, safetySettings) from the probe body is validated.

compartment.title: Explained why cache drops back to old 112K entry after warmup probe seeds 138K

compartment: Magic Context compression rewrites the conversation body between turns, changing the request prefix hash. The probe-seeded 138K cache entry becomes a miss, and Google falls back to an older 112K partial match from a previous compression boundary. This cannot be fixed in the plugin because MC modifies the request before the plugin intercepts fetch().

compartment.title: Confirmed warmup probe working, reviewed remaining cache opportunities

compartment: User confirmed the v1.6.49 warmup probe fix works — returns status=200 (aborted after first chunk). The assistant reviewed all fixed strategies (prefix stability, warmup probe, warm-cache account selection, proactive rotation) and identified Magic Context compression as the final remaining cache bust source. Proposed a post-compression re-probe as a ~1-hour remaining plugin-side win (re-seeds cache after MC compression reduces total tokens), or stopping here since the remaining issues are MC-level.

compartment.title: Investigated 99%→0% cache drop on same account with no account switch

compartment: User shared logs showing account 18 going from 99% Cache HIT (first request after warmup probe) to 0% Cache MISS (next request) with no account change. Tokens grew only 619 tokens — no MC compression. The assistant identified two possible root causes: (1) Google's implicit cache TTL (~5 minutes) expired between requests if there was a pause, or (2) Magic Context's Phase 1 `chat.messages.transform` changed the request prefix between turns. The specific cause could not be confirmed without knowing the time gap between the two requests.

compartment.title: Investigated 99%→0% same-account cache drop — root cause is MC per-turn reasoning stripping

compartment: User confirmed the 99%→0% cache drop happened within the same chat in under 5 minutes, ruling out the Google implicit cache TTL (~5 min) hypothesis. The assistant conducted a deep investigation of the Magic Context transform codebase, searching MC's per-turn transform pipeline. Found that `stripReasoningFromMergedAssistants` and `stripClearedReasoning` run on every turn (NOT gated behind `isCacheBusting`). Each turn adds new thinking blocks from the model response, which MC strips on the next turn — changing the message content array and producing a different prefix hash. MC creates sentinels as `{ type: "text", text: "" }` while the plugin creates sentinels as `{ text: "." }`, causing the prefix to differ at those positions between turns. The `read=0` (no partial match) occurs because the change is inside conversation messages early in the prefix — everything after the changed position is invalidated. The guidance injection hypothesis was ruled out (`protectedTags` is a static config value at 20). The fix requires changes in Magic Context (making reasoning stripping idempotent), not the plugin, because MC modifies messages before the plugin's `fetch()` interception — the plugin never sees the original thinking blocks.

compartment.title: Confirmed MC per-turn cache-busting root cause from logs and filed GitHub issue #125

compartment: Analyzed Magic Context logs from `C:\Users\Expiren\AppData\Local\Temp\opencode\magic-context\magic-context.log` to confirm the per-turn cache-busting root cause. Three consecutive transforms showed `reasoning replay: cleared=8→8→10`, `stripClearedReasoning: strippedParts=8→8→10`, and `reasoning cleanup: cleared=2 watermark=2437→2467` — proving each turn's new thinking blocks are stripped on the next turn, changing the prefix hash. Filed GitHub issue cortexkit/magic-context#125 with full root cause analysis, MC log evidence, three proposed fix approaches (stable sentinels, watermark-stable stripping, one-time strip at generation), and reproduction steps.

compartment.title: Patched local MC bundle to make reasoning stripping idempotent

compartment: User asked if the issue could be fixed locally since Magic Context is installed in the app. The assistant initially said no (MC transforms before the plugin's fetch intercept), but the user clarified they meant patching the MC bundle itself. Patched the local MC bundle at `C:\Users\Expiren\.cache\opencode\packages\@cortexkit\opencode-magic-context@latest\node_modules\@cortexkit\opencode-magic-context\dist\index.js`. The fix modifies `stripClearedReasoning()` to strip ALL thinking/reasoning parts to sentinels on every turn (removing the `isCleared` guard that only stripped previously-cleared parts). Before: parts with non-`[cleared]` thinking text were skipped, producing different output each turn as the watermark advanced and new thinking blocks appeared. After: every thinking/reasoning part becomes `{ type: "text", text: "" }` (via `makeSentinel()`) regardless of cleared status, making the output idempotent across turns. This is safe because Claude generates fresh thinking each turn and the Antigravity plugin already strips all thinking blocks in `request.ts`. The patch will be overwritten on MC auto-update; GitHub issue #125 tracks the upstream fix. U: i mean fix this issue in the mc transform we have the magic context already User clarified the intent was to patch the local MC bundle directly, not wait for an upstream fix.
ENVIRONMENT: * Magic Context log file: `C:\Users\Expiren\AppData\Local\Temp\opencode\magic-context\magic-context.log` (corrected path with `opencode\magic-context\` subdirectory). * Magic Context local bundle: `C:\Users\Expiren\.cache\opencode\packages\@cortexkit\opencode-magic-context@latest\node_modules\@cortexkit\opencode-magic-context\dist\index.js`.
KNOWN_ISSUES: * MC per-turn reasoning stripping (`stripClearedReasoning`, `stripReasoningFromMergedAssistants`) has a local patch applied to the MC bundle making it idempotent (all thinking parts → sentinel every turn). The patch is overwritten on MC auto-update. GitHub issue cortexkit/magic-context#125 tracks the upstream fix.
